feynman.0: Waves don’t just go straight — they curl around corners, and where two of them land in the same spot they add together. That first trick, diffraction, puts a hard ceiling on what any wave can ever show you a picture of. The second one, which Young set up with his two little slits, made a pattern of stripes that finally pinned light down as a wave.
feynman.1: Diffraction is just this: a wave spreading out into the space behind a gap or behind an obstacle — the wavefronts fanning off to the side instead of marching straight on. And notice, it’s only a change of direction. The wavelength, the frequency, the speed — none of those budge. Every wave does it — sound, water waves, light — they all spread out when they slip past an edge.
feynman.2: How much does a wave fan out? It comes down to a single comparison: the wavelength λ measured against the width w of the gap (or the size of whatever obstacle is in the way).
feynman.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave barely notices the gap — it goes pretty much straight through, with just a little fraying at the edges. Make the gap narrower (or let λ get bigger) and the spreading grows. Once λ ≥ w, the gap acts like a single point source, and the wavefronts coming out fan into near-semicircles that fill up the whole region beyond, like you see in Figure 7.1. This is exactly why you can hear someone talking through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m coming through a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, so the sound bends way out into the room — but for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, so the light just runs in straight lines and leaves you sharp shadows.
feynman.4: An obstacle does the very same thing, only backwards: the wave wraps around it and fills in the space behind, so an obstacle only casts a clean shadow if it’s a whole lot bigger than λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m bends right around a hill and reaches you on the far side; a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m doesn’t — it leaves a dead spot behind that same hill.
feynman.5: Diffraction is what sets the limit on what you can image. A wave can only tell you about an object if the object disturbs it — but a wave just bends around anything much smaller than its own wavelength almost as if nothing were there, leaving no shadow, no echo, no trace of the detail. So visible light, with wavelengths running from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, simply can’t make out anything much smaller than that, no matter how fancy the microscope. Plenty of viruses, smaller than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, are flat-out invisible to it. Want to see finer detail? You need a smaller wavelength.
feynman.6: Key idea A wave can’t resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction wipes out the information about that detail. Smaller stuff means you need a smaller λ.
feynman.7: Connects This is the whole reason electron microscopes exist. Over in 12 · Matter waves it turns out electrons have wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so an electron beam can show you individual atoms that no light microscope could ever catch.
feynman.8: Back in 1803 Thomas Young took light from a single source, sent it through two narrow slits, and looked at what landed on a screen. The particle idea of the day said you should get two bright strips, one behind each slit — simple enough. But that’s not what showed up. Instead there was a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark fringes — including dark spots sitting right where light from either slit on its own could perfectly well have reached.
feynman.9: The setup is what makes it work. Both slits are lit by the same source, so the waves leaving them are coherent: same frequency, and a phase difference that stays put. Two separate lamps can’t pull this off — their phase difference jitters around fast and at random, so whatever pattern there is just smears away before you can see it. And each slit is made narrow too, about as wide as λ, so the light diffracts hard at each one. Now the two slits behave like two coherent point sources, and their waves overlap all the way across the screen.
feynman.10: Wherever those overlapping waves meet, what happens depends on the path difference — the difference in how far the light travelled from each slit to get to that point, like in Figure 7.2. If that path difference works out to a whole number of wavelengths, the waves show up in step, add together, and the spot is bright. If it’s an odd number of half wavelengths, then crest lands on trough, the waves wipe each other out, and the spot is dark.
feynman.11: Right at the centre of the screen, the same distance from both slits, the path difference is zero — a whole number of wavelengths with n = 0 — so you always get a bright central maximum sitting there. Walk outward from the middle and the path difference climbs steadily: the first dark fringe turns up where it hits ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and on it goes.
feynman.12: Trap In both formulas n starts counting from zero. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe has path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe has (m − ½)λ — so if someone asks about the third dark fringe, they want 2.5λ. Write 3.5λ and you’ve made the classic counting slip.
feynman.13: This pattern is the clincher for light being a wave. Superposition — two disturbances adding up to reinforce each other in some places and cancel each other out completely in others — is something only waves do. Sound from two loudspeakers, water waves from two dippers: you get exactly the same fringe geometry. Particles just can’t cancel. Send two streams of particles into the same point and you could never, ever make darkness there.
feynman.14: Connects You really trust a model when it predicts something nobody saw coming. The wave model nails this pattern — but in 11 · The photon that very same model runs into an experiment it just can’t explain.
feynman.15: Near the middle of the pattern the fringes come out evenly spaced. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance between the centres of two neighbouring bright fringes — or, just as well, between two neighbouring dark ones; the two spacings come out the same, as Figure 7.3 shows.
feynman.16: Each piece does its own thing. A longer wavelength stretches the pattern out: red fringes sit wider apart than blue ones, so bumping up λ bumps up Δx. Slide the screen further back and you magnify the whole thing: a bigger L means a bigger Δx. Push the slits apart, though, and the fringes crowd together: a bigger d actually shrinks Δx, because d sits down in the denominator.
feynman.17: Flip it around to λ = Δx d/L and this same bit of geometry turns into a measuring instrument: from three lengths you can read off with a ruler, Young pinned down the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — and he did it a hundred years before anyone had any idea photons existed.
feynman.18: Ishva sends a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits separated by 0.40 mm. She treats the slits as two coherent point sources and watches the pattern land on a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula does the job.
feynman.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
feynman.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Swap in a red laser with its longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and you’d open this spacing up wider.
feynman.22: Trap Δx = λL/d throws together lengths of wildly different sizes: λ shows up in nm, d in mm, L in m. Turn every length into metres before you plug anything in — leave d as 0.40 instead of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ and your answer comes out 1000 times too small. And always check whether the answer makes sense: fringe spacings on a lab bench are millimetres, not metres.

sagan.0: Waves bend around corners, and where they overlap they add. That first habit, diffraction, sets a hard and unyielding limit on what any wave can ever show us; the second, staged in Young’s double-slit experiment, painted the striped pattern that first persuaded us light is a wave.
sagan.1: Diffraction is the spreading of a wave into the region behind a gap or an obstacle — the gentle fanning of the wavefronts away from straight-line travel. It is a change of direction and nothing more: the wavelength, the frequency and the speed of the wave all remain exactly as they were. Every wave does this — sound, water waves and light all spread when they pass an edge.
sagan.2: How far a wave spreads is decided by a single comparison: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap (or the size of the obstacle).
sagan.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave sails almost straight through, with only the faintest spreading at the edges. As the gap narrows, or as λ grows, the spreading widens; and once λ ≥ w, the gap becomes a single point source and the emerging wavefronts open into near-semicircles, flooding the whole region beyond, as shown in Figure 7.1. This is why a conversation carries through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m through a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, so the sound bends deep into the room — while for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, and the light marches in straight lines and casts sharp shadows.
sagan.4: An obstacle does the same thing in reverse: the wave curls around it and fills in behind, so an obstacle throws a clean shadow only when it is far larger than λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m diffracts around a hill and is heard on the far side; a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m leaves a silent dead spot behind that very same hill.
sagan.5: Diffraction draws the ultimate boundary on what we can see. A wave can only carry word of an object if that object disturbs it — yet a wave flows almost untouched around anything much smaller than its own wavelength, leaving no shadow, no echo, no trace of the detail at all. Visible light, with wavelengths from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, therefore cannot resolve structures much smaller than this, however fine the microscope we build: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, remain forever invisible to it. To see finer detail, we must reach for a smaller wavelength.
sagan.6: Key idea A wave can never resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction simply erases the information about that detail. Finer detail demands a smaller λ.
sagan.7: Connects This very limit is the reason electron microscopes exist at all. In 12 · Matter waves electrons turn out to carry wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so a beam of them can image individual atoms that no light microscope could ever hope to glimpse.
sagan.8: In 1803 Thomas Young let light from a single source pass through two narrow slits and watched what arrived on a screen. The particle picture of his day foretold two bright strips, one behind each slit. What appeared instead was a long procession of evenly spaced bright and dark fringes — including darkness in places that light from either slit alone could plainly reach.

Two separate lamps can never manage this — their phase difference shifts swiftly and at random, so any pattern they make flickers away too fast to be seen. Each slit is also drawn narrow, close to λ in width, so that the light diffracts strongly at each: the two slits then act as two coherent point sources whose waves overlap across the entire screen.
sagan.10: Wherever the overlapping waves meet, the outcome turns on the path difference — the difference between the distances travelled from each slit to that point, as shown in Figure 7.2. If the path difference is a whole number of wavelengths, the waves arrive in step, add constructively, and the point shines bright. If it is an odd number of half wavelengths, crest falls upon trough, the waves cancel, and the point goes dark.

Move outward from the centre and the path difference grows steadily: the first dark fringe arrives where it reaches ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and so onward.

Particles cannot cancel one another; two streams of particles arriving together at a single point could never conjure darkness there.

Visible light, with wavelengths from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, therefore cannot resolve structures much smaller than this, however fine the microscope: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, are simply invisible to it. To see finer detail, you need a smaller wavelength.
cox.6: Key idea A wave cannot resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction quietly erases any information about that detail. Smaller detail asks for smaller λ.
cox.7: Connects This is the whole reason electron microscopes exist. In 12 · Matter waves electrons turn out to carry wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so an electron beam can image individual atoms that no light microscope could ever hope to see.
cox.8: In 1803 Thomas Young took light from a single source, passed it through two narrow slits, and watched what arrived on a screen. The particle model of his day predicted two bright strips, one behind each slit. What appeared instead was a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark fringes — and, remarkably, dark fringes sitting in places that light from either slit alone could perfectly well reach.
cox.9: The arrangement is everything. The two slits are lit by the same source, so the waves leaving them are coherent: they share the same frequency and hold a constant phase difference. Two independent lamps simply cannot manage this — their phase difference flickers fast and at random, so any pattern would shift far too quickly to catch. Each slit is also made narrow, close to λ, so the light diffracts strongly at each one: the two slits then behave as two coherent point sources, whose waves overlap right across the screen.
cox.10: Where the overlapping waves arrive, the outcome hangs on the path difference — the difference between the distances travelled from each slit to that point, as shown in Figure 7.2. If the path difference is a whole number of wavelengths, the waves arrive in step, add constructively, and the point is bright. If it is an odd number of half wavelengths, crest meets trough, the waves cancel, and the point is dark.
cox.11: At the centre of the screen, equidistant from both slits, the path difference is zero — a whole number of wavelengths with n = 0 — so the pattern always carries a bright central maximum. Move out from the centre and the path difference grows steadily: the first dark fringe appears where it reaches ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and so on outward.
cox.12: Trap In both formulas n counts from zero. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe has path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe has (m − ½)λ — so a question about the third dark fringe is asking for 2.5λ; writing 3.5λ is the classic counting slip.
cox.13: This pattern is the decisive evidence that light is a wave. Superposition — two disturbances adding to give reinforcement in some places and complete cancellation in others — is something only waves do: sound from two loudspeakers and water waves from two dippers produce exactly the same fringe geometry. Particles cannot cancel; two streams of particles arriving at the same point could never, ever produce darkness there.
cox.14: Connects A model earns our trust when it predicts a result nobody saw coming. The wave model accounts beautifully for this pattern; in 11 · The photon that very same model runs into an experiment it cannot explain.
cox.15: Near the centre of the pattern the fringes are evenly spaced. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance between the centres of adjacent bright fringes — or, just as well, between adjacent dark fringes; the two spacings come out the same, as shown in Figure 7.3.
cox.16: Each variable plays its own part. A longer wavelength spreads the pattern out: red fringes sit wider than blue, so increasing λ increases Δx. Sliding the screen further away magnifies the whole pattern: increasing L increases Δx. And pushing the slits apart squeezes the fringes together: increasing d decreases Δx, because d sits in the denominator.
cox.17: Rearranged as λ = Δx d/L, the same geometry quietly becomes a measuring instrument: from three lengths you could take with a ruler, Young pinned down the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a full century before anyone knew the first thing about photons.
cox.18: Ishva sends a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits separated by 0.40 mm. She models the slits as two coherent point sources and watches the pattern form on a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula applies.
cox.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
cox.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Swap in a red laser of longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing would open out wider.
cox.22: Trap Δx = λL/d mixes lengths of wildly different sizes: λ arrives in nm, d in mm, L in m. Convert every length to metres before you substitute — leaving d as 0.40 instead of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ makes the answer 1000 times too small. And always sense-check the result: fringe spacings on a laboratory bench are millimetres, not metres.

greene.0: Waves do two things that seem almost too simple to matter, and yet between them they reshape how we picture light itself. They bend around corners, and where two of them overlap they add. The first behaviour—diffraction—places a hard ceiling on what any wave can ever reveal. The second, staged in Young’s elegant double-slit experiment, painted a pattern of stripes across a screen that, once and for all, declared light to be a wave.
greene.1: Diffraction is the spreading of a wave into the region behind a gap or an obstacle—the way wavefronts fan out and abandon straight-line travel. Notice what it is not: it is a change of direction and nothing more. The wavelength, the frequency and the speed all carry on unchanged. And it is universal—sound, water waves and light all spread when they brush past an edge. Every wave diffracts.
greene.2: Just how much a wave spreads comes down to a single, beautifully simple comparison: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap—or, equally, the size of the obstacle.
greene.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave sails almost straight through, fraying only a little at the edges. But narrow the gap, or stretch the wavelength, and the spreading grows; once λ ≥ w, the gap surrenders entirely—it behaves as a single point source, and the emerging wavefronts billow out into near-semicircles, flooding the whole region beyond, as shown in Figure 7.1. This is precisely why a conversation reaches you through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m passing through a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, so the sound bends generously into the room—whereas for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, and the light marches in straight lines, casting sharp shadows.
greene.4: An obstacle plays the same game in reverse: the wave curls around it and knits itself back together behind, so an obstacle throws a clean shadow only when it dwarfs λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m diffracts around a hill and reaches you on the far side; a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m, passing the very same hill, leaves a dead spot in its wake.
greene.5: Here diffraction draws a line that no instrument can cross. A wave can only tell you about an object if that object disturbs it—but a wave glides almost untouched around anything much smaller than its own wavelength, leaving no shadow, no echo, no trace of the detail. So visible light, with wavelengths running from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, simply cannot resolve structures much finer than this, however magnificent the microscope: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, are invisible to it. To see finer detail, you need a shorter wavelength.
greene.6: Key idea A wave cannot resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength—diffraction erases all information about that detail. Finer detail demands a smaller λ.
greene.7: Connects This very limit is the reason electron microscopes exist at all. In 12 · Matter waves electrons reveal wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so an electron beam can image individual atoms that no light microscope could ever hope to see.

If it is an odd number of half wavelengths, crest meets trough, the waves annihilate one another, and the point falls dark.

greene.14: Connects A model earns our trust when it predicts something we had not anticipated. The wave model accounts beautifully for this pattern; yet in 11 · The photon that same model walks into an experiment it simply cannot explain.

greene.22: Trap Δx = λL/d throws together lengths of wildly different scales: λ arrives in nm, d in mm, L in m. munroe.10: Where the two waves land on top of each other, what you get depends on the path difference — how much further the light from one slit travelled than the light from the other to reach that exact point, as shown in Figure 7.2. If that extra distance is a whole number of wavelengths, the two waves arrive in step, add up, and the spot is bright. If it’s an odd number of half wavelengths, a crest lands on a trough, the two waves cancel out, and the spot is dark.
munroe.11: Right in the middle of the screen you’re the same distance from both slits, so the path difference is zero — a whole number of wavelengths, with n = 0 — which is why there’s always a bright central maximum sitting there. Walk outward from the centre and the path difference climbs steadily: it hits ½λ at the first dark fringe, 1λ at the next bright fringe, and so on up.
munroe.12: Trap In both formulas n starts at zero. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe has path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe has (m − ½)λ — so when a question asks about the third dark fringe, it wants 2.5λ. Writing 3.5λ is the classic off-by-counting mistake.
munroe.13: This pattern is the clincher for light being a wave. Superposition — two disturbances adding up to reinforce each other in some places and wipe each other out completely in others — is a thing only waves do: sound from two loudspeakers and water waves from two dippers make the exact same fringe geometry. Particles can’t cancel. Two streams of particles arriving at the same point can pile up, but they can never add up to darkness.
munroe.14: Connects You accept a model when it correctly predicts something you weren’t expecting. The wave model nails this pattern; in 11 · The photon the very same model runs into an experiment it flat-out cannot explain.
munroe.15: Near the centre of the pattern the fringes are evenly spaced out. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance from the centre of one bright fringe to the centre of the next one — or, just as well, from one dark fringe to the next; the two spacings come out the same, as shown in Figure 7.3.
munroe.16: Each variable does its own thing, independently. A longer wavelength stretches the pattern out: red fringes sit further apart than blue ones, so bigger λ means bigger Δx. Sliding the screen further away blows the whole pattern up: bigger L means bigger Δx. Pushing the slits apart crams the fringes together: bigger d shrinks Δx, because d is on the bottom of the fraction.
munroe.17: Flip it around to λ = Δx d/L and the same geometry turns into a ruler for measuring wavelength. From three lengths you can measure with an actual ruler, Young pinned down the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a hundred years before anyone knew photons existed.
munroe.18: Ishva shines a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits 0.40 mm apart. She treats the slits as two coherent point sources and looks at the pattern on a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula is fair game.
munroe.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
munroe.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Swap in a red laser with a longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing gets wider.
munroe.22: Trap Δx = λL/d throws together lengths of wildly different sizes: λ shows up in nm, d in mm, L in m. Convert every length to metres before you plug anything in — leave d as 0.40 instead of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ and your answer comes out 1000 times too small. Then sanity-check the result: fringe spacings on a lab bench are millimetres, not metres.

bronowski.0: Waves bend around corners, and where they meet they add. The first of these habits, diffraction, draws a hard and inescapable line around what any wave can ever show us; the second, brought into the open by Young in his double slit, painted the row of bright and dark stripes that settled, at last, the old question and declared light to be a wave.
bronowski.1: Diffraction is the spreading of a wave into the ground that lies behind a gap or an obstacle — the fanning out of the wavefronts away from the straight path they would otherwise keep. It is a turning, and nothing more: the wavelength, the frequency, the speed of the wave are all left untouched. And it is universal — sound, water, light, every wave there is spreads when it brushes past an edge.
bronowski.2: How far a wave spreads is decided by a single comparison, and only one: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap, or against the size of the obstacle it must round.
bronowski.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave goes through all but straight, fraying only a little at the edges. Narrow the gap, or let λ grow, and the spreading widens; and once λ ≥ w the gap acts as a single point, and the wavefronts coming through it open into near-semicircles that fill the whole region beyond, as shown in Figure 7.1. This is why a voice carries through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m passing a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, so the sound bends far into the room — whereas for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, and the light keeps to straight lines and throws sharp shadows.
bronowski.4: An obstacle does the same thing turned about: the wave bends around it and closes in behind, so that a body casts a clean shadow only when it dwarfs λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m diffracts around a hill and is heard on the far side; a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m leaves a dead spot behind that very same hill.
bronowski.5: Diffraction sets the boundary on what we can see. A wave can carry word of an object only if the object disturbs it — yet a wave bends past anything much smaller than its own wavelength almost as though it were not there, leaving no shadow, no echo of the fine detail. Visible light, with wavelengths running from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, can therefore resolve nothing much smaller than this, however fine the microscope we build: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, lie forever beyond its reach. To see finer than that, we must go to a smaller wavelength.
bronowski.6: Key idea A wave cannot resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction wipes away the news of that detail. To see finer, we need a smaller λ.
bronowski.7: Connects This very limit is the reason the electron microscope was ever built. In 12 · Matter waves electrons turn out to carry wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so a beam of them can image the single atom that no light microscope will ever bring into view.
bronowski.8: In 1803 Thomas Young let light from a single source fall through two narrow slits and watched what arrived on a screen. The particle picture of his day foretold two bright bands, one behind each slit. What came instead was a long, even row of bright and dark fringes — and among them, dark fringes standing exactly where light from either slit on its own could plainly reach.
bronowski.9: The arrangement is everything. Both slits are lit by the same source, so the waves that leave them are coherent: one frequency between them, and a phase difference that holds steady. Two separate lamps could never manage it — their phase difference shifts swiftly and at random, so any pattern they made would race away before the eye could hold it. Each slit, too, is cut narrow, near the size of λ, so that the light diffracts strongly as it passes: the two slits then stand as two coherent point sources, their waves overlapping across the whole screen.
bronowski.10: Where the overlapping waves come together, the outcome turns on the path difference — the gap between the distances travelled from each slit to that point, as shown in Figure 7.2. If that gap is a whole number of wavelengths, the waves arrive in step, add together, and the point shines bright. If it is an odd number of half wavelengths, crest meets trough, the waves undo one another, and the point falls dark.
bronowski.11: At the very middle of the screen, the same distance from both slits, the path difference is nothing at all — a whole number of wavelengths with n = 0 — and so the pattern always holds a bright central maximum. Move outward from the centre and the path difference climbs steadily: the first dark fringe stands where it reaches ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and so on outward.
bronowski.12: Trap In both formulas n is counted from zero. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe carries path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe (m − ½)λ — so a question about the third dark fringe is asking for 2.5λ, and to write 3.5λ is the old, familiar slip in the counting.
bronowski.13: This pattern is the evidence that decided the matter, and decided it for the wave. Superposition — two disturbances adding to give reinforcement in one place and utter cancellation in another — is a thing only waves can do: sound from two loudspeakers, water waves from two dippers, throw down exactly the same geometry of fringes. Particles cannot cancel; two streams of particles meeting at one point could never, by any contrivance, make darkness there.
bronowski.14: Connects A model earns our trust when it foretells a result no one looked for. The wave model accounts for this pattern; and yet in 11 · The photon the same model is brought face to face with an experiment it cannot explain.
bronowski.15: Near the middle of the pattern the fringes stand evenly spaced. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance from the centre of one bright fringe to the centre of the next — or, just as truly, between adjacent dark ones; the two spacings come out the same, as shown in Figure 7.3.
bronowski.16: Each quantity does its work on its own. A longer wavelength opens the pattern out: red fringes sit wider than blue, so to raise λ is to raise Δx. Carry the screen further off and the whole pattern is magnified: to raise L is to raise Δx. Drive the slits apart and the fringes crowd together: to raise d is to lower Δx, for d sits in the denominator.
bronowski.17: Set down again as λ = Δx d/L, this same geometry becomes an instrument of measurement: out of three lengths a ruler can reach, Young drew the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a full century before anyone had so much as heard of the photon.
bronowski.18: Ishva sends a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits parted by 0.40 mm. She takes the slits as two coherent point sources and watches the pattern fall on a screen 1.8 m away, so that L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula holds good.
bronowski.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
bronowski.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Trade the laser for a red one of longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing would widen.
bronowski.22: Trap Δx = λL/d gathers together units of wildly different size: λ comes in nm, d in mm, L in m. Turn every length into metres before you substitute — leave d as 0.40 in place of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ and the answer falls 1000 times too small. And test the result against good sense: fringe spacings on a laboratory bench are measured in millimetres, never in metres.

Sliding the screen further away magnifies the whole thing: bigger L, bigger Δx. But push the slits apart and the fringes crowd together: bigger d shrinks Δx, because d is sitting downstairs in the denominator.
mack.17: Rearrange it as λ = Δx d/L and the same geometry quietly turns into a measuring instrument: from three lengths you can read off a ruler, Young pinned down the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a full century before anyone had heard of a photon.
mack.18: Ishva shines a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits separated by 0.40 mm. She treats the slits as two coherent point sources and watches the pattern land on a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula is fair game.
mack.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
mack.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Swap in a red laser with its longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing would open right up.
mack.22: Trap Δx = λL/d throws together lengths of wildly different sizes: λ shows up in nm, d in mm, L in m. Convert every length to metres before you substitute — leave d as 0.40 instead of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ and your answer lands 1000 times too small. levin.0: Waves bend around corners, and where they meet they add. The first habit, diffraction, draws a hard boundary on what any wave can ever show us; the second, set loose in Young’s double-slit experiment, painted the stripes that first revealed light to be a wave.
levin.1: Diffraction is the way a wave spills into the space behind a gap or an obstacle — the wavefronts spreading sideways, leaving the straight path behind. Nothing else changes: the wavelength, the frequency, the speed all hold steady. Only the direction gives. And every wave does it — sound, water, light — each one spreads when it meets an edge.
levin.2: How far a wave fans out comes down to a single contest: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap — or the size of the obstacle in its way.
levin.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave slips almost straight through, fraying only a little at the edges. Narrow the gap, or lengthen λ, and the spreading grows; and once λ ≥ w, the gap turns into a single point source — the emerging wavefronts swell into near-semicircles, flooding the whole region beyond, as shown in Figure 7.1. This is why a voice carries through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m through a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, so the sound bends deep into the room — while for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, and the light holds its straight lines and casts sharp shadows.
levin.4: An obstacle plays the same game in reverse: the wave curls around it and closes in behind, so an obstacle throws a clean shadow only when it dwarfs λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m bends around a hill and reaches the far side; a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m leaves a dead spot behind that very same hill.
levin.5: Diffraction is what sets the ceiling on seeing. A wave can only carry word of an object if the object disturbs it — but a wave glides almost untouched around anything far smaller than its own wavelength, leaving no shadow, no echo of the detail. Visible light, with wavelengths from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, can therefore never resolve structures much finer than this, no matter how fine the microscope: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, stay invisible to it. To image finer detail, you need a shorter wave.
levin.6: Key idea No wave can resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction erases all word of it. Finer detail demands a smaller λ.
levin.7: Connects This ceiling is the reason electron microscopes exist. In 12 · Matter waves electrons turn out to carry wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so an electron beam can picture individual atoms that no light microscope will ever reach.
levin.8: In 1803 Thomas Young sent light from a single source through two narrow slits and watched what landed on a screen. The particle picture of the day promised two bright strips, one behind each slit. What arrived instead was a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark fringes — with darkness sitting in places that light from either slit alone could plainly have reached.
levin.9: The arrangement is everything. Both slits drink from the same source, so the waves leaving them are coherent: one frequency, one fixed phase difference held between them. Two separate lamps could never manage this — their phase difference flickers fast and at random, and any pattern would shudder away before you could see it. Each slit is cut narrow too, close to λ, so the light diffracts hard at each one: the two slits then stand as two coherent point sources whose waves overlap clear across the screen.
levin.10: Where the overlapping waves arrive, the outcome turns on the path difference — the gap between the distances travelled from each slit to that point, as shown in Figure 7.2. If the path difference is a whole number of wavelengths, the waves arrive in step, add constructively, and the point shines. If it is an odd number of half wavelengths, crest meets trough, the waves erase each other, and the point goes dark.
levin.11: At the centre of the screen, equally far from both slits, the path difference is zero — a whole number of wavelengths with n = 0 — so the pattern always opens with a bright central maximum. Move outward, and the path difference climbs steadily: the first dark fringe arrives where it reaches ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and onward.
levin.12: Trap In both formulas n counts from zero. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe carries path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe carries (m − ½)λ — a question about the third dark fringe is asking for 2.5λ; writing 3.5λ is the classic miscount.
levin.13: This pattern is the deciding evidence for the wave nature of light. Superposition — two disturbances adding to reinforce in some places and cancel utterly in others — is something only waves do: sound from two loudspeakers, water from two dippers, both lay down exactly this fringe geometry. Particles cannot cancel; two streams of particles meeting at a single point could never conjure darkness there.
levin.14: Connects A model earns belief when it predicts something no one saw coming. The wave model owns this pattern; in 11 · The photon the same model walks into an experiment it cannot explain.
levin.15: Near the centre of the pattern the fringes fall evenly apart. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance between the centres of neighbouring bright fringes — or, just as truly, between neighbouring dark ones; the two spacings come out the same, as shown in Figure 7.3.
levin.16: Each variable speaks on its own. A longer wavelength opens the pattern out: red fringes sit wider than blue, so raising λ raises Δx. Sliding the screen further away magnifies the whole thing: raising L raises Δx. Pushing the slits apart pinches the fringes together: raising d lowers Δx, because d sits in the denominator.
levin.17: Rearranged as λ = Δx d/L, the same geometry becomes an instrument. From three lengths a ruler can read, Young drew out the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a full century before anyone had heard of photons.
levin.18: Ishva sends a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits set 0.40 mm apart. She treats the slits as two coherent point sources and watches the pattern fall on a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula holds.
levin.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
levin.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Swap in a red laser of longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing would widen.
levin.22: Trap Δx = λL/d gathers up lengths of wildly different sizes: λ comes in nm, d in mm, L in m. czerski.0: Waves don’t just barrel along in straight lines — they curl round corners, and where two of them overlap they pile up on top of each other. czerski.1: Diffraction is what happens when a wave spills into the region behind a gap or an obstacle — the way the wavefronts fan out sideways instead of marching dead straight ahead. czerski.2: How far a wave fans out comes down to a single head-to-head comparison: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap — or against the size of the obstacle it’s squeezing past.
czerski.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave sails almost straight through, with just a little fraying at the edges. czerski.4: An obstacle plays the same game in reverse: the wave wraps around it and closes in behind, so a barrier only casts a clean shadow if it’s far bigger than λ. czerski.7: Connects This very limit is the reason electron microscopes exist at all. In 12 · Matter waves electrons turn out to carry wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so an electron beam can pick out individual atoms that no light microscope could ever hope to see.

attenborough.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
attenborough.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Replace the laser with a red one of longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing would open out wider still.
attenborough.22: Trap Δx = λL/d brings together units of wildly different sizes: λ arrives in nm, d in mm, L in m. Convert every length to metres before substituting — leaving d as 0.40 instead of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ makes the answer 1000 times too small. And always weigh the result for plausibility: fringe spacings on a laboratory bench are millimetres, never metres.

greekepic.0: Waves spread about the corners of the world and add their voices where they meet. The first of these wonders, diffraction, lays a hard bound upon what any wave may behold; the second, set forth in the twin-slit ordeal of Young, wove the striped pattern that crowned light forever as a wave.
greekepic.1: Diffraction is the spreading of a wave into the realm behind a gap or an obstacle — the turning of the wavefronts away from their straight and arrow-true path. It is a change of bearing only: the wavelength, the frequency, and the speed of the wave abide unchanged. Every wave bends thus — sound, the waters, and light itself all spread as they pass an edge.
greekepic.2: By how much a wave spreads is ruled by a single reckoning: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap (or the girth of the obstacle).
greekepic.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave passes well-nigh straight onward, with but slight spreading at the edges. As the gap is narrowed (or λ swells), the spread grows; and once λ ≥ w, the gap bears itself as a point source, and the emerging wavefronts spread into near-semicircles, filling the whole realm beyond, as shown in Figure 7.1. So it is that speech carries through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m through a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, and the sound bends far into the chamber — while for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, and the light journeys in straight lines, casting shadows keen-edged.
greekepic.4: An obstacle works the selfsame wonder reversed: the wave bends about it and fills the void behind, so that an obstacle casts a clean shadow only if it tower far greater than λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m bends about a hill and is heard upon its far side; yet a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m leaves a dead and silent place behind that same hill.
greekepic.5: Diffraction sets the bound upon all seeing. A wave may carry tidings of a thing only if that thing trouble its passage — but a wave bends almost untouched about whatsoever is far smaller than its own wavelength, leaving no shadow and no echo of the fine detail. Visible light, whose wavelengths run from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, can therefore make out nothing much smaller than this, however noble the microscope: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, lie hidden from its gaze. To behold finer detail one must wield a smaller wavelength.
greekepic.6: Key idea A wave cannot be wielded to resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction strips away all knowledge of that detail. Smaller detail demands smaller λ.
greekepic.7: Connects This bound is the very reason electron microscopes were born. In 12 · Matter waves electrons prove to bear wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so that an electron beam may image single atoms that no light microscope shall ever behold.
greekepic.8: In the year 1803 Thomas Young drew light from a single source through two narrow slits and watched what came to pass upon a screen. The particle creed of that age foretold two bright strips, one behind each slit. But what appeared instead was a long array of evenly spaced bright and dark fringes — among them dark fringes in places that light from either slit alone might reach.
greekepic.9: The ordering of it matters. The two slits are kindled by the same source, so the waves departing them are coherent: they bear the same frequency and a phase difference held ever constant. Two lamps standing apart can work no such thing — their phase difference shifts swiftly and at random, so any pattern flees too fast for the eye to hold. Each slit is likewise wrought narrow, near to λ, that the light may bend strongly at each: the two slits then bear themselves as two coherent point sources whose waves overlap across the whole screen.
greekepic.10: Where the overlapping waves arrive, the outcome hangs upon the path difference — the difference between the distances travelled from each slit to that place, as shown in Figure 7.2. If the path difference be a whole number of wavelengths, the waves arrive in phase, add constructively, and the place shines bright. If it be an odd number of half wavelengths, crest meets trough, the waves cancel, and the place falls dark.
greekepic.11: At the centre of the screen, equidistant from both slits, the path difference is naught — a whole number of wavelengths with n = 0 — so the pattern ever bears a bright central maximum. Faring outward from the centre, the path difference grows steadily: the first dark fringe rises where it reaches ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and onward so.
greekepic.12: Trap Mark well: in both formulas n counts from zero. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe bears path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe (m − ½)λ — a question after the third dark fringe awaits 2.5λ; to write 3.5λ is the ancient counting blunder.
greekepic.13: This pattern is the deciding testimony for the wave nature of light. Superposition — two disturbances adding to give reinforcement in some places and utter cancellation in others — is conduct that waves alone display: sound from two loudspeakers and water waves from two dippers raise the selfsame fringe geometry. Particles cannot cancel one another; two streams of particles arriving at the same place could never beget darkness there.
greekepic.14: Connects A model is taken into honour when it foretells a result none had looked for. The wave model accounts for this pattern; in 11 · The photon that same model meets an ordeal it cannot explain.
greekepic.15: Near the centre of the pattern the fringes stand evenly spaced. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance between the centres of neighbouring bright fringes — or as truly between neighbouring dark fringes; the two spacings are one and the same, as shown in Figure 7.3.
greekepic.16: Each quantity works its will alone. A longer wavelength spreads the pattern wide: red fringes sit wider than blue, so to increase λ is to increase Δx. To bear the screen further away magnifies the whole pattern: to increase L is to increase Δx. To drive the slits apart presses the fringes together: to increase d lessens Δx, for d dwells in the denominator.
greekepic.17: Recast as λ = Δx d/L, the same geometry becomes an instrument of measure: from three lengths a ruler may take, Young divined the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a full century before aught was known of photons.
greekepic.18: Ishva sends a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits parted by 0.40 mm. She models the slits as two coherent point sources and beholds the pattern upon a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula holds.
greekepic.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
greekepic.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Were the laser replaced by a red one of longer wavelength, or the slit separation halved, this spacing would widen.
greekepic.22: Trap Δx = λL/d mingles units of widely differing measure: λ comes in nm, d in mm, L in m. Turn every length to metres before substituting — to leave d as 0.40 in place of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ renders the answer a thousandfold too small. The result should be weighed for plausibility: fringe spacings upon a laboratory bench are millimetres, not metres.

cnpop.0: 波这玩意儿，遇到拐角会绕过去，叠在一起还会互相加成。第一种行为叫衍射，它给所有的波都画下了一条死线——你能成多清楚的像，到此为止；第二种行为，被杨氏双缝实验请上了舞台，搞出了一排条纹图样，正是这排条纹一锤定音，把光的“波”身份给坐实了。
cnpop.1: 衍射，说白了就是波钻过一道缝、或者绕过一个障碍物之后，往后面那块区域里铺开的现象——波前不再老老实实走直线，而是朝两边散开。注意，它只改变方向：波长、频率、波速一个都没变。而且别以为这是光的专利——所有的波都会衍射，声音、水波、光，统统都会在经过边缘时散开。
cnpop.2: 波到底铺得有多开，全看一个比较：波长 λ 跟缝宽（或者障碍物尺寸）w 谁大谁小。
cnpop.3: 当 λ/w ≪ 1 的时候，波基本上直挺挺地穿过去，顶多在边缘那儿稍微散一点点。可一旦缝越收越窄（或者 λ 越长越大），散开的程度就蹭蹭往上涨；等到 λ ≥ w，这道缝直接化身一个点源，冒出来的波前散成接近半圆的样子，把后面整片区域全占满，如图 7.1 所示。这就是为啥隔着一道门也能听见人说话：波长 0.75 m 的声音穿过 0.90 m 宽的门，λ/w = 0.83，声音于是一路拐进屋里——可换成绿光，λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷，光就老老实实走直线，留下边界锐利的影子。
cnpop.4: 障碍物的玩法是反着来的同一套：波绕到它后面、把背影那块给填上，所以一个障碍物想投出一道干净利落的影子，它就得比 λ 大上一大截才行。波长 3.0 × 10² m 的 AM 无线电信号能绕过一座小山、在山背后照样收到；而波长 0.15 m 的手机信号，到了同一座山背后就只剩一片信号死角。
cnpop.5: 衍射给“成像”这件事划了天花板。波想要捎带回某个物体的信息，前提是这物体得能扰动它——可问题来了，碰上比自己波长小得多的东西，波几乎原封不动地绕过去，既不留影子也不留半点回声，细节就这么被吞了。可见光的波长在 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m 到 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m 之间，所以再牛的显微镜也别想分辨出比这小得多的结构：很多病毒尺寸不到 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m，对它来说就是隐形的。想看清更细的细节？那就得换更小的波长。
cnpop.6: Key idea 一种波，没法分辨远比它自己波长小的细节——衍射会把那些细节的信息直接抹掉。想看更小的细节，就得上更小的 λ。
cnpop.7: Connects 电子显微镜的存在，理由就在这儿。在 12 · Matter waves 里你会发现，电子的波长比可见光短上成千上万倍，所以一束电子能给一个个原子成像，而这是任何光学显微镜这辈子都办不到的事。
cnpop.8: 1803 年，托马斯·杨让光从单一光源出发，穿过两道窄缝，然后盯着屏幕上的结果看。当时流行的粒子模型给出的预言是：两条亮带，一道缝后面一条。结果屏幕上冒出来的，却是一长排明暗相间、间距均匀的条纹——而且某些暗条纹偏偏出现在那种“单独哪道缝的光都能照到”的位置上。
cnpop.9: 这套装置的讲究可大了。两道缝是被同一个光源照亮的，所以从缝里出来的波是相干的：频率相同，相位差恒定不变。换两盏各管各的灯就玩不转了——它们的相位差变得又快又乱，任何图样都晃得太快，根本来不及看。另外每道缝都做得很窄，窄到跟 λ 一个量级，好让光在每道缝那儿都使劲衍射：这么一来，两道缝就摇身一变，成了两个相干的点源，它们的波在整个屏幕上铺开、互相重叠。
cnpop.10: 重叠的波抵达某个点时，结果如何，就看路程差了——也就是从两道缝分别走到那个点的距离之差，如图 7.2 所示。要是路程差正好是波长的整数倍，两列波同相抵达、相长叠加，这个点就亮。要是它是半波长的奇数倍，波峰撞上波谷、互相抵消，这个点就暗。
cnpop.11: 在屏幕正中央，到两道缝的距离一样远，路程差为零——按 n = 0 算，是波长的整数倍——所以这套图样的正中间永远顶着一条明晃晃的中央极大。从中心往外挪，路程差稳稳地往上涨：第一条暗纹出现在它够到 ½λ 的地方，下一条亮纹出现在它够到 1λ 的地方，以此类推。
cnpop.12: Trap 划重点别踩坑：两条公式里的 n 都是从零开始数的。中央极大是 n = 0 那条亮纹（路程差 0λ），第一条暗纹则是 n = 0（路程差 ½λ）。从中央极大往外数，第 m 条亮纹的路程差是 mλ，第 m 条暗纹的路程差是 (m − ½)λ——问你第三条暗纹，它要的答案是 2.5λ；要是写成 3.5λ，那就是教科书级别的数错坑。
cnpop.13: 这套图样，就是光具有波动性的决定性铁证。叠加——两个扰动加到一块儿，有些地方互相增强、有些地方彻底抵消——这种行为，只有波才玩得出来：两个扬声器发出的声音、两个振子激起的水波，搞出来的条纹几何跟这个一模一样。粒子可没法互相抵消；两股粒子流抵达同一个点，绝不可能在那儿憋出一片黑暗。
cnpop.14: Connects 一个模型啥时候被接受？当它预言出一个原本没人料到的结果的时候。波动模型把这套图样解释得明明白白；可到了 11 · The photon，同一个模型就撞上了一个它怎么也解释不了的实验。
cnpop.15: 在图样靠中心的那块儿，条纹的间距是均匀的。条纹间距 Δx 指的就是相邻两条亮纹中心之间的距离——换成相邻两条暗纹之间也行，这两个间距是一样的，如图 7.3 所示。
cnpop.16: 每个变量都各管各的，互不掺和。波长越长，图样铺得越开：红色条纹排得比蓝色宽，所以 λ 一增大，Δx 跟着增大。把屏幕往远处一搬，整套图样就被放大：L 一增大，Δx 也增大。可把两道缝往两边一掰开，条纹反倒被挤到一块儿：d 一增大，Δx 反而减小，因为 d 蹲在分母上。
cnpop.17: 把它改写成 λ = Δx d/L，同样这套几何关系就摇身一变，成了一把测量尺：靠三段拿尺子就能量出来的长度，杨就把可见光的波长给算出来了——几千万分之一米这么个数——而这比人类对光子有任何了解，足足早了一个世纪。
cnpop.18: Ishva 拿一支波长 532 nm 的绿色激光笔，打过一对间距 0.40 mm 的缝。她把两道缝当成两个相干点源，在 1.8 m 开外的屏幕上观察图样，于是 L ≫ d，条纹间距公式正好用得上。
cnpop.19: 公式：Δx = λLd，其中 λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m，d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m。
cnpop.21: 答案：Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm。要是把激光笔换成波长更长的红色那支，或者把缝间距砍掉一半，这个间距还会再拉宽。
cnpop.22: Trap Δx = λL/d 这条公式把好几种尺寸差老远的单位混在一锅里：λ 是按 nm 给的，d 是按 mm，L 是按 m。代入之前，先把每段长度统统换算成米——要是把 d 留成 0.40 而不是 4.0 × 10⁻⁴，算出来的答案就会小上 1000 倍。结果还得回头掂量一下合不合理：实验台上的条纹间距是毫米级别的，可不是米级别的。

ai.0: Waves do two things that seem almost mischievous: they curl around corners, and where two of them overlap they add together. The first trick, diffraction, quietly sets a hard ceiling on what any wave can ever show you. The second, staged so beautifully in Young’s double-slit experiment, painted a row of stripes onto a screen — and those stripes settled the argument that light is a wave.
ai.1: Diffraction is what happens when a wave fans out into the region behind a gap or an obstacle — the wavefronts spreading away from the straight line they were travelling. Notice that this is only a change of direction: the wavelength, frequency and speed all stay exactly as they were. And every wave does it — sound, water waves, light, all of them bend and spread the moment they slip past an edge.
ai.2: Just how much a wave spreads comes down to one single comparison: the wavelength λ set against the width w of the gap (or the size of the obstacle).
ai.3: When λ/w ≪ 1 the wave barrels almost straight through, fraying only a little at the edges. Narrow the gap (or stretch λ) and the spreading grows; and once λ ≥ w, the gap starts acting like a single point source, the emerging wavefronts spreading out into near-semicircles that flood the whole region beyond, as shown in Figure 7.1. This is exactly why a conversation drifts through a doorway: for sound of wavelength 0.75 m passing a 0.90 m doorway, λ/w = 0.83, so the sound bends far into the room — whereas for green light, λ/w = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ ÷ 0.90 = 6.1 × 10⁻⁷, and the light marches in straight lines, casting sharp-edged shadows.
ai.4: An obstacle plays the same game, just in reverse: the wave wraps around it and fills in the space behind, so an obstacle only throws a clean shadow when it is much larger than λ. An AM radio signal of wavelength 3.0 × 10² m bends right around a hill and reaches you on the far side; a mobile-phone signal of wavelength 0.15 m, by contrast, leaves a dead spot tucked behind the very same hill.
ai.5: Diffraction is also what caps our ability to see. A wave can only carry information about an object if that object disturbs it — but a wave slides almost untouched around anything much smaller than its own wavelength, leaving no shadow, no echo, no trace of the detail. So visible light, with wavelengths running from 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, simply cannot resolve structures much finer than that, no matter how superb the microscope: many viruses, less than 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m across, stay flatly invisible to it. Want to see finer detail? You need a smaller wavelength.
ai.6: Key idea A wave can’t resolve detail much smaller than its own wavelength — diffraction simply wipes out the information about that detail. Finer detail demands a smaller λ.
ai.7: Connects This very limit is the reason electron microscopes exist at all. In 12 · Matter waves electrons turn out to carry wavelengths thousands of times shorter than visible light, so an electron beam can image individual atoms that no light microscope could ever hope to see.
ai.8: In 1803 Thomas Young took light from a single source, sent it through two narrow slits, and watched what landed on a screen. The particle picture of his day made a confident prediction: two bright strips, one behind each slit. What showed up instead was a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark fringes — with dark fringes sitting at places that light from either slit on its own could happily reach.
ai.9: The setup is doing careful work here. Both slits are lit by the same source, so the waves leaving them are coherent: same frequency, and a phase difference that holds steady. Two separate lamps could never pull this off — their phase difference jitters fast and at random, so any pattern would flicker away before you could see it. Each slit is also cut narrow, comparable to λ, so the light diffracts strongly at each one: the two slits then behave as two coherent point sources whose waves overlap right across the screen.
ai.10: Wherever the overlapping waves meet, the outcome hangs on the path difference — the gap between the distances each wave has travelled from its slit to that point, as shown in Figure 7.2. If that gap is a whole number of wavelengths, the waves arrive in step, add constructively, and the spot lights up bright. If it’s an odd number of half wavelengths, crest lands on trough, the waves cancel, and the spot goes dark.
ai.11: Right at the centre of the screen, the same distance from both slits, the path difference is zero — a whole number of wavelengths with n = 0 — so there is always a bright central maximum sitting there. Step outward from the centre and the path difference climbs steadily: the first dark fringe shows up where it hits ½λ, the next bright fringe where it reaches 1λ, and so on out across the screen.
ai.12: Trap In both formulas n counts from zero — don’t lose track of that. The central maximum is the n = 0 bright fringe (path difference 0λ), and the first dark fringe is n = 0 (path difference ½λ). Counting outward from the central maximum, the m-th bright fringe has path difference mλ and the m-th dark fringe has (m − ½)λ — so a question about the third dark fringe is after 2.5λ; writing 3.5λ is the classic counting slip.
ai.13: This pattern is the clincher for the wave nature of light. Superposition — two disturbances adding to reinforce in some spots and cancel completely in others — is something only waves ever do: sound from two loudspeakers and water waves from two dippers trace out exactly the same fringe geometry. Particles simply can’t cancel; two streams of particles meeting at a point could never, ever conspire to produce darkness there.
ai.14: Connects A model earns its keep when it predicts something nobody expected. The wave model nails this pattern — yet in 11 · The photon that same model walks straight into an experiment it cannot explain.
ai.15: Near the middle of the pattern the fringes are spaced evenly. The fringe spacing Δx is the distance between the centres of neighbouring bright fringes — or, just as well, between neighbouring dark fringes; the two spacings come out the same, as shown in Figure 7.3.
ai.16: Each variable pulls its own lever. A longer wavelength stretches the pattern out: red fringes sit wider than blue, so cranking up λ increases Δx. Sliding the screen further away magnifies the whole pattern: increasing L increases Δx. And pushing the slits apart squeezes the fringes together: increasing d decreases Δx, because d sits down in the denominator.
ai.17: Flip it around to λ = Δx d/L and the same geometry quietly becomes a measuring instrument: from three lengths you can read off a ruler, Young pinned down the wavelength of visible light — a few ten-millionths of a metre — a full century before anyone knew the first thing about photons.
ai.18: Ishva aims a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm through a pair of slits separated by 0.40 mm. She treats the slits as two coherent point sources and watches the pattern land on a screen 1.8 m away, so L ≫ d and the fringe-spacing formula applies.
ai.19: Formula: Δx = λLd with λ = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m and d = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m.
ai.21: Answer: Δx = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m = 2.4 mm. Swap in a red laser of longer wavelength, or halve the slit separation, and this spacing would widen out.
ai.22: Trap Δx = λL/d throws together units of wildly different sizes: λ comes in nm, d in mm, L in m. Convert every length to metres before you substitute — leave d as 0.40 instead of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ and your answer lands 1000 times too small. Always sanity-check the result: fringe spacings on a lab bench are millimetres, not metres.
